blurb:
Jacquelyn came into rescue from animal control and landed in a foster home where she has been, by all accounts, an absolute delight. She is about two years old, around 58 pounds, and her foster describes her as lovable, huggable, and genuinely fun to have around. She is the kind of dog who wiggles her way into your lap without being pushy about it, and she has learned to dial it back when asked.

She is a Velcro dog through and through. She wants to be near her people, knows basic cues like sit and back, and her foster says nothing gets past her on the alert front. She loves playing with the other dogs in her foster home, so if you have a friendly dog at home already, a meet and greet is a great first step. No cats, and probably no toddlers she might accidentally bowl over with enthusiasm.

Jacquelyn does have a congenital kidney condition, which is worth knowing. Her foster says it is easily managed and does not affect her day-to-day quality of life at all. She just needs a few potty breaks during the day, so a work-from-home setup or flexible schedule would be ideal. She sleeps through the night without any issues. Mostly Mutts is based in Kennesaw. Start the adoption process through their website.
scoring_notes:
• Affectionate, trained, and foster-proven with other dogs — a strong candidate for remote-working households who want a devoted companion
• Congenital kidney condition handled with candor creates a compelling underdog narrative that invites reader empathy and action
• No mention of prior adoption and clear management needs suggest she has been waiting for a specifically suited home, implying moderate shelter time

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Ladybug came in from animal control with her siblings, spent time in a foster home, and recently moved to the Kitty Aquarium at the Mostly Mutts shelter. She's about two months old and just over two pounds, so she's got some growing left to do.

She already has her whole personality figured out, though. During her photo shoot she made a strong impression, and volunteers noticed she'll stick her paw out to get your attention if you're petting one of the other kittens and accidentally skip her. She knows what she wants and she's not shy about it. She's social, litter trained, and gets along great with the other cats she's lived with.

She'll need her kitten shot series finished as she grows, and her spay will be required and scheduled once she's big enough. She's already microchipped. If you have another cat at home who could use a playful, assertive little buddy, Ladybug is worth a visit.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. You can email adopt@mostlymutts.org or call to arrange a meet.
scoring_notes:
• Two-month-old kitten with a bold, attention-grabbing personality is among the most instantly adoptable profiles in the group
• The detail of her tapping a paw to demand attention is a charming, shareable anecdote that will delight newsletter readers
• Recently arrived from animal control into the shelter floor, suggesting she has had very little wait time and urgency is low

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Iceman was adopted from Mostly Mutts as a kitten, and his family's situation changed in a way that meant he had to come back. That's it. No drama, no behavior problems. He's five years old now, about 11 pounds, and fully up to date on everything: shots, neuter, microchip, combo tested negative.

He likes hanging out with his people and watching the world go by. Low key, affectionate, the kind of cat who's just happy to be in the room with you. He was around four kids in a previous foster home and loved every one of them, so if you've got gentle kids at home, that's probably going to work out fine.

One thing worth knowing: he's not a fan of other male cats. A female cat at home is a different story and could actually be a great fit. Dogs are a question mark since he hasn't been around them much, but a meet and greet can help sort that out.

His adoption fee is $175. Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Email adopt@mostlymutts.org or give them a call to get the process started.
scoring_notes:
• Low-maintenance, kid-friendly adult cat with a clean health record is a strong fit for a broad range of households
• Returned through no fault of his own at age five — a quiet, sympathetic story that will resonate with readers who value loyalty
• Five years old and back in rescue signals he has likely been waiting a meaningful stretch, adding urgency for the right adopter

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Gina came in as part of a Go-Gos themed litter, got adopted, and then got returned about a month later because she started having some stomach issues. She's been in foster care since, working through food changes and vet visits, and she's doing better. Nobody's figured out exactly why she vomits sometimes. She probably always will, at least a little. That's the honest truth about her.

Here's the other honest truth: her foster mom calls her the best cat ever. She's about 11 months old, weighs around five pounds, and is likely to stay small. Playful, sweet, independent enough to do her own thing but absolutely loves to snuggle when she's in the mood. She lived with two adult cats in foster and did great with them, and she met a leashed dog without flinching.

Because of her special diet, it's easiest if she's the only pet eating in the house, or if you can manage separate feeding. It's a small logistical thing, not a dealbreaker. If you've been wanting a cat that still feels like a kitten well past kittenhood, Gina is genuinely that cat. She just needs someone willing to roll with her quirks.

Mostly Mutts is based in Kennesaw. Reach out to adopt@mostlymutts.org or call to set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• Small, kitten-like cat with a sweet personality makes her highly relatable to a wide audience
• Returned adoption + honest vomiting disclosure creates a refreshingly transparent, emotionally resonant story
• Has cycled through adoption and foster care with unresolved health mystery, suggesting a prolonged search for the right home

blurb:
Josie has floppy ears, a little nubby tail, and enough energy to keep up with pretty much anyone who enjoys actually doing things. She is a six-year-old hound mix, about 35 pounds, and her foster puts her energy level at a seven or eight out of ten. But here is the thing: her cuddle level matches. She wants the adventure AND the couch time after.

She was adopted once before and came back not because anything was wrong with her, but because the energy mismatch was real. She needs people who genuinely want an active dog, not one who mostly naps. She does some mouthy, playful nibbling during play, never aggressive, just enthusiastic. Older kids around ten and up would probably mesh well with her vibe.

Josie does best as an only dog and no cats. She has been through the Mostly Mutts training program and her foster says she has learned a lot. She just needs the right active household to finally stick. If you hike, run, or just spend a lot of time outside and want a dog who is going to be thrilled about every single outing, go meet Josie. Mostly Mutts can set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• High-energy hound with a matching cuddle drive is a compelling pitch for active readers who want a true adventure partner
• A prior return due to energy mismatch — not behavior — reframes her as misunderstood rather than difficult, which builds reader advocacy
• Training program completion plus a returned adoption history suggests she has been in the rescue system long enough to warrant featured urgency

blurb:
Yasmeen is eight years old and has already done the work. She went through a prison foster and training program, spent time learning to trust, and came out the other side as a loyal, loving dog who her foster mom says will make her adopters feel like they hit the jackpot. That is not hype. That is someone who has lived with her.

She is not the dog who trusts everyone immediately, and that is actually a feature. She takes a little time to decide you are good people, and once she does, she is all in. She does some guarding when strangers come into her space, so the recommendation is to meet new people outside the home first. It is a manageable routine, not a dealbreaker. She is good with cats and friendly dogs, and she has done well with kids around age seven and ten. Older kids or teenagers would probably be the best fit given her protective instincts.

What you get with Yasmeen is a dog who has been through a lot, figured out how to trust again, and is ready to settle in somewhere safe and stay. She is 58 pounds of medium-energy loyalty. Mostly Mutts can walk you through the adoption process and set up a meet with her trainer if needed.
scoring_notes:
• Prison program graduate who rebuilt trust and emerged loyal — an extraordinary redemption arc that will move newsletter readers to act
• At eight years old with selective trust and guarding behaviors, her adopter profile is narrow, signaling she has almost certainly waited a long time
• Her foster's 'jackpot' endorsement paired with her age and history creates maximum urgency — this is the feature story the newsletter needs
